artifacts.content:
Mom,

I’m finally sitting down with a quiet house and a lukewarm cup of tea, so I figured I’d actually write instead of just sending you pictures of the kids.

Ethan’s been on my mind a lot. You remember how rattled he was after that March 2024 lockdown drill at Riverside Middle? The “this is not a drill” announcement, the lights off, kids crying in the corner of my own 6th‑grade ELA room… he still tenses up when the intercom crackles. I’ve been trying to channel that into something creative with my STEM Makerspace Storytelling Lab idea, almost like “Ethan’s Anxiety Stories,” so he and the other kids who freaked out that day have a way to process it besides just more talking.

At the same time, I keep thinking about you. It still feels surreal that in Fall 2023 we went from “Mom’s just a little forgetful” to sitting in that office in San Marcos hearing “early‑onset Alzheimer’s.” I’ve been toying with this “Maggie’s Memory Makerspace” concept in my head, using storytelling and simple circuits the way we did those step‑by‑step practice boards after your stroke at Seton in May 2022. I don’t know if it’s too personal, but it helps me not feel so helpless.

Day‑to‑day life is the usual circus—Daniel’s buried in projects at Hill Country Civil Design Group again, Ethan’s soccer, Lily’s piano, me grading essays on the couch while a podcast hums in the background. I’ll be in San Marcos next Saturday for groceries and your rheumatology follow‑up. Text me if you think of anything you need from H‑E‑B besides the usual yogurt and those crackers you like.

Love you,
Melissa

Hey Hannah,

Catching you between lesson planning and Lily insisting we make slime “for science.”

Money’s been weighing on me, so I’m going to overshare. Remember when Daniel’s hours got cut at Hill Country Civil Design Group last Spring 2024? We’re still digging out from that mess. At one point I tallied it up and realized we were sitting on about $18,000 in credit card debt. I half‑joked to Daniel about a project called “Overdue Bills & Budgeting Tales” because I keep turning our money panic into little math‑and‑story problems for my kids at Riverside.

Work has been… a lot. Back in March 2023, when those 6th‑grade parents went after my ELA curriculum (the usual “too political / too dark” complaints), I ended up in the ER here in Riverside with what I thought was a heart attack. Turned out to be a severe anxiety attack. They hooked me up to monitors, did the whole chest‑X‑ray routine, and then sent me home with breathing exercises and a giant bill. I keep thinking about a “Panic to Prose” unit where kids turn their own scary moments into narratives and builds, but toned way down, obviously.

Meanwhile Ethan’s doing better, mostly, but he still has rough patches. Since Dr. McKay at Riverside Behavioral Health formally diagnosed him with ADHD and dyslexia back in March 2024 and the campus diagnostician finished his IEP, I’ve been rethinking everything about how I assess my 6th graders. His whole section at Riverside Middle is kind of my test case for this STEM‑ELA mashup.

Anyway, how’s clinic life? Want to come up one Friday for Tex‑Mex and patio time? I’ll even let you beat me at Catan.

Love,
M

WORKING PROJECT BRAIN DUMP – DO NOT SHARE

1. Ethan + lockdown drill
- March 2024 Riverside Middle School lockdown drill (“this is not a drill” message, kids crying under desks in my 6th‑grade ELA room).
- Ethan’s anxiety spiked after that; still jumpy with loudspeaker and fire alarms.
- Idea seed: “Ethan’s Anxiety Stories” – have students build calm‑down devices / safe‑room models with microcontrollers and then narrate their experiences.

2. Mom / Maggie’s memory
- Fall 2023 early‑onset Alzheimer’s diagnosis for Mom (Margaret “Maggie” Thompson) in San Marcos.
- Plus her minor stroke at Seton Medical Center in May 2022 – I made those step‑by‑step tech practice boards for her PT.
- “Maggie’s Memory Makerspace” concept: kids create interactive timelines / memory boxes for fictional grandparents dealing with dementia.

3. Truancy + “repair tutoring”
- October 2023: started after‑school “repair tutoring” for 7th graders failing ELA because of chronic absenteeism tied to court‑ordered visitation.
- Wrote weekly behavior/attendance summaries for Assistant Principal Mark Delgado to go with truancy court packets.
- Student J.’s truancy hearing on November 14, 2023 at the Williamson County Justice Center really crystallized the need for alternative demonstrations of mastery.

4. Domestic violence / safe house model
- Spring 2022–April 2023: increase in family crises.
- March 8, 2023: 8th grader’s father arrested for domestic violence during dismissal outside the Riverside Middle School bus loop.
- April 2023 pilot: Dr. Alvarez at Riverside Family Counseling and I agreed that student could build/code a “safe house” instead of writing about the incident.

Through‑line: kids need hands‑on, tech‑infused storytelling options when traditional essays are retraumatizing, inaccessible, or just not realistic given home situations.

Dan,

I know we’ve talked pieces of this, but I want it all in one place for both of us.

Ever since the campus lost the dedicated computer lab after those January 2023 budget cuts, my classroom has basically been a traveling circus of borrowed Chromebooks. Half the time the cart is booked, so we end up sharing my personal Chromebook and that used Canon Vixia camcorder I grabbed on Facebook Marketplace for $120 back in February 2023. I’ve been dumping all the kids’ video drafts onto my private Google Drive because there’s no district server space.

The part that really bothers me is how mixed it all is. On the same Drive where I’ve got their Romeo and Juliet green‑screen projects, I also have Ethan’s ADHD evaluation reports from Dell Children’s Medical Center, plus old tax docs, our mortgage stuff, Lily’s recital videos. It’s a privacy nightmare waiting to happen.

And then there’s Ethan himself. Remember Spring 2024 when he almost failed 6th‑grade ELA? After Dr. Patel at Riverside Pediatric Associates adjusted his anxiety meds on March 5, 2024, he basically shut down on any timed writing for three weeks. The only way I got him to talk about The Giver was that night at the dining room table here at 2147 Willow Bend Drive, when we let him prototype that little microcontroller “mood meter” scene. He could explain everything out loud while he wired LEDs, then finally script it.

All of that—plus Mom’s memory stuff and your hours roller coaster—has me doubling down on getting more sustainable tech in my room. I’m tired of patching everything together with our personal devices and nerves.

Love,
M

metadata.subject: Why I’m obsessed with fixing my classroom tech

artifacts.content:
Event: Friday Debrief + Planning Block
Time: Friday, 3:30–5:30 p.m.
Location: Room 214, Riverside Middle School
Attendees: Melissa (me), sometimes Javier pops in

Notes / running agenda:

1. 8th‑grade PBL cohort
- Check in on student who had the panic attack in my 4th period ELA back in September 2023.
- Review her 504 plan with Ms. Ramirez’s notes: limit oral presentations, avoid unstructured group work.
- Adjust Makerspace Storytelling Lab roles so she can be scriptwriter or designer instead of presenter.

2. “Frequent flyers” / tech misuse kids
- Revisit October 2023 discipline referrals: vaping in bathrooms, explicit content on school iPads.
- Assistant Principal Mr. Delgado wants those 6th–7th graders to have structured leadership roles.
- Draft “tech manager” and “digital ethics lead” job descriptions for group projects.

3. Foster students / unstable housing
- Among my ~126 students, four are currently in foster care, in two group homes off FM 1431.
- One 7th grader moved there in June 2024 after CPS removed him from his parents’ home on Willow Creek Drive for neglect.
- Make sure all major creative work (filming, coding, editing) can be completed during class or open lab hours; no assumptions about home tech access.

4. Newcomer ELs
- Plan next week’s scaffolded storyboard templates for the three recent arrivals from Matamoros and Reynosa.
- Quietly keep J.M.’s situation (dad detained by ICE April 2023, now with aunt at Riverside Oaks Apartments) in mind when choosing prompts.

5. Self‑care
- Block 10 min at the end to breathe, stretch, and actually pack up by 5:30 so I’m not reliving March 2023 ER‑level anxiety.

metadata.title: Friday Work Debrief – Student Needs Focus

artifacts.content:
Hannah 🟢

Hannah: You alive? Haven’t heard from you since Lily’s recital pics.

Me: Barely. This week at Riverside has been… a lot.

Hannah: What now?

Me: Remember the kid whose dad got arrested for domestic violence outside the bus loop on March 8, 2023? The one Dr. Alvarez from Riverside Family Counseling started seeing?

Hannah: Yeah, the safe house model kid.

Me: Yep. He’s back in my 8th‑grade PBL cohort and we’re expanding that “safe house” idea with microcontrollers. Trying to give him a way to process without reliving the whole arrest.

Hannah: That’s heavy.

Me: And then there’s my truancy crew. Since October 2023 I’ve been doing those after‑school “repair tutoring” sessions for 7th graders missing ELA because of court‑ordered visitation. I was writing weekly behavior/attendance summaries for Mark Delgado to go to Williamson County truancy court. J.’s hearing on Nov 14, 2023 at the Williamson County Justice Center just gutted me.

Hannah: You’re basically a teacher/social worker.

Me: It feels like it. On top of that, I’m juggling the four foster kids in my classes who are in those group homes off FM 1431, plus Ethan’s stuff, plus Mom’s memory sliding since the Fall 2023 Alzheimer’s diagnosis.

Hannah: Have you talked to anyone since the March 2023 ER anxiety thing?

Me: No. I keep saying I will, then I stay late in Room 214 tweaking group roles so my vaping/iPad “frequent flyers” from October 2023 can be “digital ethics leads” instead of hallway terrors.

Hannah: I’m free Sunday. Call me after the kids are in bed.

Me: Deal. I’ll bring tea, you bring the tough love.

Hi Javier,

Long brain dump ahead—sorry in advance.

I’ve been sketching out how the STEM Makerspace Storytelling Lab can better support our most fragile groups. Between your 7th‑graders and my sections, we’re looking at about 126 kids total, and the needs are all over the place.

For 6th grade, I keep coming back to Ethan’s class. After Dr. Lauren McKay at Riverside Behavioral Health formally diagnosed him with ADHD and dyslexia in March 2024 and the campus diagnostician finalized his IEP, I realized how many of his friends in that section have similar profiles. If we bake the accommodations (audiobooks, speech‑to‑text, visual storyboards) into the makerspace projects, it helps everyone, not just the kids with paperwork.

Your 7th‑grade crew has its own layers. The three newcomers from Matamoros and Reynosa—especially J.M., living with his aunt at Riverside Oaks Apartments since his dad was detained by ICE in April 2023—need low‑pressure ways to practice English. I’m imagining bilingual captioning on their videos and letting them storyboard in Spanish first.

Then there are the four foster kids between our rosters, all in those group homes off FM 1431. One of mine was moved there in June 2024 after CPS pulled him from Willow Creek Drive for neglect. I’m designing timelines so all filming, coding, and editing can be done in class or during open lab hours; no assuming reliable Wi‑Fi or quiet space at “home.”

Add in the vaping/explicit‑content “frequent flyers” Mr. Delgado flagged last October 2023, and I’m more convinced than ever we need structured roles like “tech manager” and “digital ethics lead” to give them a way to rebuild trust.

Can we grab 20 minutes during PLC next week to align rubrics and make sure we’re not overloading any one group?

– Melissa

metadata.subject: Target groups for Makerspace Storytelling Lab
